response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a 67-year-old carpenter living in Kansas City who spends about an hour daily on social media, I've learned that managing fake content starts with being a bit skeptical but not too worried. When I see something that doesn't look right—like a video that's too smooth or a post that seems too good to be true—I just pause and think, 'Is this real?' My parents taught me to check the source, so I often go back to the original website or contact the person directly. I don't get too upset because I know the internet can be tricky, but I do take time to verify things before sharing them. For example, when I saw a post about a new type of wood that was supposed to be super strong, I checked with a local lumberyard and found out it was just a scam. I also use the 'report' button on social media sites to flag content that looks suspicious. It's important to stay calm and not let fake news get under your skin. I've found that my hobbies like baking and playing video games help me stay grounded and focused on what's real. I don't think about it too much because I know my mental health is good, and I've got a good sense of what's real from my experiences. So, I'd say the key is to be careful, check the facts, and not share anything until you're sure.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 67-year-old man with a vocational diploma, lives in Kansas City, and spends one hour daily on social media. They have good mental health, are skeptical but not overly anxious, and value practical checks (like contacting local businesses). The response draws on their background: carpentry (practical verification), parents' education (critical thinking), hobbies (baking, video games, cinema for mental grounding), and low neuroticism (calm approach). Examples are drawn from their daily life (checking with lumberyards) and align with their low openness (skepticism without overthinking) and moderate conscientiousness (systematic verification). The tone is calm, practical, and grounded in real-world experience, avoiding technical jargon. The advice is specific to their age and occupation, emphasizing verification and calmness.
response_parsed.tone: Calm, practical, grounded in real-world experience
citations: Personal experience of a carpenter verifying materials
